does: SAEmnesia introduces a supervised sparse autoencoder framework designed to identify and erase specific concepts from diffusion models by enforcing one-to-one mappings between concepts and individual neurons. The core innovation addresses feature splitting, where concepts are normally scattered across many latent features, making them difficult to isolate and remove. By systematically labeling concepts during sparse autoencoder training, the method achieves feature centralization that binds each concept to a single interpretable neuron. This targeted approach enables efficient concept erasure with dramatically reduced computational overhead. The paper demonstrates significant improvements over prior sparse autoencoder-based unlearning: a 96.67% reduction in hyperparameter search requirements, a 9.22% improvement on the UnlearnCanvas benchmark, and superior performance in sequential unlearning scenarios where nine objects are removed with 28.4% higher accuracy. The method also effectively suppresses inappropriate content on the I2P benchmark while maintaining robustness against adversarial attacks.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in concept unlearning for generative models and the application of sparse autoencoders to interpret neural networks. It directly responds to limitations of existing sparse autoencoder-based unlearning methods that struggle with feature splitting and require extensive hyperparameter tuning. The paper extends prior work on interpretability through sparse autoencoders—which decompose learned representations into interpretable basis functions—by adding a supervised component that guides neurons toward single-concept encodings. It also connects to the broader literature on machine unlearning in diffusion models, which has explored various approaches to remove unwanted generative capabilities including object removal and content suppression. The supervised training approach draws on techniques from concept bottleneck models and disentangled representation learning that similarly aim to create interpretable, well-separated concept encodings. The benchmark comparisons against UnlearnCanvas and I2P datasets anchor this work within established evaluation frameworks for unlearning effectiveness.
enables: SAEmnesia opens pathways for more precise control over generative model behavior and capabilities. The enforced concept-neuron mappings enable future work on selective capability modification where multiple concepts can be cleanly removed in sequence without catastrophic forgetting or unintended side effects. The scalability improvements suggest potential for practical deployment in applications requiring content filtering or privacy-preserving model adaptation. The interpretability gains from having explicit concept neurons could facilitate better understanding of how diffusion models encode semantic information, potentially enabling safer model design. The adversarial robustness demonstrated suggests directions for building unlearning methods resilient to adversarial perturbations. Future work could leverage these clear concept mappings for steering generation toward or away from specific concepts with fine-grained control, or for auditing and certifying that problematic concepts have been genuinely removed. The reduction in hyperparameter search burden makes concept erasure more accessible and practical, potentially enabling broader adoption in real-world systems where content control is necessary.
